text: Hey everyone. In a previous video, we demonstrated a laparoscopic low anterior resection with an end colostomy.

In today's video, we'll discuss some tips and tricks how to do a laparoscopic low anterior resection with primary anastomosis. The patient was diagnosed to have a rectal tumor at ten to eleven centimeters from the anal verge. We plan to do a low anterior resection with a primary anastomosis.
The assistant holds the vascular pedicle taut while the surgeon begins dissection at the sacral promontory, ensuring optimal exposure for the laparoscopic low anterior resection.

The right-handed surgeon holds an energy device in the right hand and elevates the pedicle with the left hand, assisted by additional traction. The dissection begins at the sacral promontory, proceeding both cephalad and caudad, splitting the peritoneum to expose the root of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle. Scissors are used for this maneuver, though any energy device could be utilized.

A steep head and down position here is absolutely crucial. If you can identify the duodenum, which is not always possible in larger patients, the dissection direction becomes much easier as you can head toward the origin of the vascular pedicle in the direction of the duodenum.

The surgeon lifts the vascular pedicle and identifies the loose areolar tissue between the superior hypogastric pre-aortic plexus and the root of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), ensuring the dissection plane is parallel to the aorta.
The surgeon carefully delineates the origin of the inferior mesenteric artery, gradually exposing the vessel within the surgical field.

The surgeon creates a window underneath the pedicle of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and inferior mesenteric vein (IMV), facilitating the identification of the ureter on the opposite side, especially in larger patients, before transecting the vascular pedicle.

The surgeon uses plastic clips or a vascular stapler to divide the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) based on the pedicle and patient size. Following the division, the dissection proceeds from medial to lateral towards the fascia of Toldt, focusing on the mesocolon of the retroperitoneal structure. This approach aims to facilitate the subsequent lateral to medial dissection during colon mobilization.
The surgeon focuses on the top of the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, applying traction and counter traction to ensure entry into the correct TME plane for a bloodless dissection.

The surgeon begins lateral dissection after establishing a posterior TME plane, splitting the anterior peritoneum while carefully preserving the hypogastric, inferior epigastric, and superior epigastric plexuses.

The surgeon encounters a large loop of redundant sigmoid colon obstructing the pelvic view. To improve visibility, they perform an early lateral dissection to mobilize the sigmoid colon and pull it out of the pelvic cavity, facilitating further rectal dissection.
The surgeon uses a fan retractor to facilitate low pelvic dissection, aiming to reach four to five centimeters below the tumor, approximately six centimeters from the anal verge.

The surgeon demonstrates the right hypogastric nerve emerging from the superior hypogastric plexus. The bilobed appearance of the TME plane is visible, indicating correct dissection. The assistant pulls the mobilized rectum out of the pelvic cavity to provide a clear view of the lower TME planes for continued dissection under tension.
The surgeon performs medial to lateral mobilization from right to left, preparing to open the left to right plane. The mobilization is demonstrated as relatively easy for a right-handed surgeon.

The surgeon performs a low anterior dissection, dividing the mesorectum approximately six centimeters from the anal verge using an energy device. The dissection is carried out without extending to the pelvic floor, utilizing the natural tapering of the TME plane.

The surgeon, using a right-handed approach, divides the right side of the mesorectum and performs posterior and anterior dissection before proceeding with the left-sided division at the site of the chosen anastomosis.

The surgeon mobilizes the left colon, extending the dissection up to the splenic flexure. The mesocolon is visibly separated from the retroperitoneal structures, forming the pedicle package as described by Bill Heald.

The surgeon ensures complete mobilization of the left colon, meticulously taking down each strand to maximize mobility for the anastomosis. The anvil of the surgical stapler is placed in the proximal left colon, and the surgeon checks that there is sufficient length for the colon to reach the pelvis before inserting the stapler from below.

After transecting the rectum during the low anterior resection, the rectal stump visibly springs back towards the pelvic floor. The surgeon checks for any twist in the bowel and performs a leak test, which shows no leakage. The surgical field appears clear, indicating adequate resection.
Thank you for watching and please subscribe to watch further similar videos.
